1: direct My heart overflows with a pleasing theme; I address my verses to the king; my tongue is like the pen of a ready scribe. The royal wedding song that follows finds its ultimate subject in Christ the King. Hebrews 1:8-9 applies verses 6-7 directly to Christ, transforming this court hymn into a Christological confession. The psalmist's overflow of inspiration is the Spirit's own testimony to the Son.
2: shadow You are the most handsome of the sons of men; grace is poured upon your lips. Christ is the beautiful one, the one in whom grace is poured — full of grace and truth (John 1:14). The crowds marveled at the gracious words coming from his mouth (Luke 4:22). The beauty of the king is the beauty of holiness and love made visible in the Incarnation.
3: shadow Gird your sword on your thigh, O mighty one, in your splendor and majesty! The divine warrior imagery reaches Christ: From his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations (Revelation 19:15). But his sword is the word — the Word of God who rides forth (Revelation 19:13), whose splendor is the glory of the resurrection.
4: shadow In your majesty ride out victoriously for the cause of truth and meekness and righteousness; let your right hand teach you awesome deeds! Christ rides in the cause of truth, meekness, and righteousness — his own marks: I am the truth (John 14:6); I am gentle and lowly in heart (Matthew 11:29); the righteous one (1 John 2:1). The awesome deeds are the cross and resurrection.
5: shadow Your arrows are sharp in the heart of the king's enemies; the peoples fall under you. The victory of Christ over spiritual enemies: the reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8). At the cross the powers are disarmed (Colossians 2:15); at the parousia the peoples fall before the one who rides on the white horse (Revelation 19:11-16).
6: direct Your throne, O God, is forever and ever. The scepter of your kingdom is a scepter of uprightness. Hebrews 1:8 quotes this verse directly to the Son: But of the Son he says, Your throne, O God, is forever and ever, the scepter of uprightness is the scepter of your kingdom. The Father addresses Christ as God — a direct Christological affirmation of the Son's deity and eternal reign.
7: direct You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness; therefore God, your God, has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your companions. Hebrews 1:9 continues the citation: the anointing of the Son by the Father is his eternal glad anointing as Messiah (Christ = Anointed One). The Spirit descending on Jesus at baptism (Matthew 3:16) fulfills the eternal decree of this verse.
8: shadow Your robes are all fragrant with myrrh and aloes and cassia. The fragrant robes appear in Christ's anointing: Mary's costly nard (John 12:3), the myrrh and aloes at his burial (John 19:39-40). The king's fragrance fills both his life and death — the aroma of Christ (2 Corinthians 2:15) that spreads from death to life and life to death.
9: shadow Daughters of kings are among your ladies of honor; at your right hand stands the queen in gold of Ophir. The royal court imagery looks forward to the Bride of Christ: I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband (Revelation 21:2). The queen at the right hand is the church clothed in the righteousness of Christ (Revelation 19:8).
10: shadow Hear, O daughter, and consider, and incline your ear: forget your people and your father's house, The call to the bride to leave her former family echoes the call of discipleship: Whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me (Matthew 10:37). Christ calls his church to a love that transcends all earthly loyalties in order to be wholly his.
11: shadow and the king will desire your beauty. Since he is your lord, bow to him. Christ desires the beauty of his church — the beauty he himself imparts: so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle (Ephesians 5:27). He who is her Lord is also her bridegroom who desires what he has made beautiful through his own blood.
12: shadow The people of Tyre will seek your favor with gifts, the richest of the people. The nations bringing their glory into the city of God: the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it (Revelation 21:24). Christ is the king to whom the nations bring tribute — the universal reach of his reign extending even to Tyre, a symbol of Gentile commercial power.
13: shadow All glorious is the princess in her chamber, with robes interwoven with gold. The church's inner glory — clothed with Christ's righteousness (Galatians 3:27). The fine linen bright and pure given to the bride (Revelation 19:8) is the righteous deeds of the saints — the beauty of a people transformed by union with the king.
14: shadow In many-colored robes she is led to the king, with her virgin companions following behind her. The bridal procession: Let us rejoice and exult and give him the glory, for the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready (Revelation 19:7). The many-colored robes are the varied gifts and graces of the Spirit in the gathered church.
15: shadow With joy and gladness they are led along as they enter the palace of the king. The joy of entry into the King's presence — enter into the joy of your master (Matthew 25:23). The wedding feast parable (Matthew 22:1-14) and the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9 — blessed are those who are invited) give this verse its eschatological fullness.
16: shadow In place of your fathers shall be your sons; you will make them princes in all the earth. The multiplication of the king's family: Christ makes sons in place of the fathers (Galatians 3:29 — heirs of Abraham through faith); the princes in all the earth are the citizens of the kingdom sent as salt and light to every nation (Matthew 5:13-14; Psalms 28:19).
17: shadow I will cause your name to be remembered in all generations; therefore nations will praise you forever and ever. The name of Christ remembered in all generations — the name above every name (Philippians 2:9). The Great Commission is the mechanism: go and make disciples of all nations... baptizing them in the name (Matthew 28:19-20). Every generation and every nation brought into the praise of the King.
